In [1]:
%matplotlib inline

Marginal Fitting: Current Job Rank

Comments:

  • current_job_rank is fixed to match the proportions per age in the 2017 SynPop
  • The choice of persons to fix is done with a probability model based on their is_employed and current_edu
In [2]:
import logging
import os
import sys

from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
In [3]:

from synpop import marginal_fitting, utils
from synpop.synpop import Persons
from synpop.marginals import FSO_ActivePopPredictionsClient

logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)  # Set logging level: WARNING, INFO, DEBUG

synpop_persons.overwrite_column('current_edu', fixed_edu)

persons = synpop_persons.data[COLUMNS_OF_INTEREST].copy(deep=True)

del synpop_persons

FSO Data¶

  • In notebook 05a, the level_of_employment will be fitted to the BFS predictions.
  • In order for the cross-tables to work, the sum of current_job_rank that are either employee, management or apprentice must match the number of employed people.
In [16]:
# This is a function that is only used for this Notebook so stays here
def prepare_bfs_level_of_activity_marginal_table(bfs_avg_active_people_by_age, all_ages_set, year):
    
    prop_active = (bfs_avg_active_people_by_age.reindex(all_ages_set, fill_value=0) / 100).to_frame()
    prop_inactive = 1 - prop_active

    prop_active['is_employed'] = True
    prop_active = prop_active.reset_index().set_index(['age', 'is_employed'])
    prop_inactive['is_employed'] = False
    prop_inactive = prop_inactive.reset_index().set_index(['age', 'is_employed'])

    bfs_prop_predictions = pd.concat([prop_active, prop_inactive]).sort_index()

    bfs_prop_predictions = bfs_prop_predictions.iloc[:, 0].rename(f'expected_ratios_{year}')
    return bfs_prop_predictions

Scenario Year

In [17]:
active_pop_client = FSO_ActivePopPredictionsClient(granularity='age').load(YEAR)
active_pop_stats_by_age = active_pop_client.stats

all_ages = set(persons['age'].unique())
bfs_predicted_ratio = prepare_bfs_level_of_activity_marginal_table(active_pop_stats_by_age['avg_active_people'], all_ages, YEAR)

2020-04-22 10:10:17,759 - INFO - numexpr.utils: NumExpr defaulting to 8 threads.

FSO without job seekers¶

  • BFS predicts active people which includes job seekers.
  • In MOBi, all working people go to a job, job seekers have level_of_employment = 0.
  • Based on the BFS own definition, the unemployement rate in 2017 is about 4.5%.

References:

  • https://www.seco.admin.ch/seco/fr/home/wirtschaftslage---wirtschaftspolitik/Wirtschaftslage/Arbeitslosenzahlen.html
  • https://www.bfs.admin.ch/bfs/fr/home/statistiques/travail-remuneration/activite-professionnelle-temps-travail/personnes-actives/scenarios-population-active.assetdetail.9687328.html
In [18]:
# The very conservative assuption of 2% unemployement has been made for 2040.
UNEMPLOYMENT_RATE = 0.02

bfs_without_unemployed = bfs_predicted_ratio.copy(deep=True)
bfs_without_unemployed.loc[:, True] = bfs_without_unemployed.loc[:, True].values * (1 - UNEMPLOYMENT_RATE)
bfs_without_unemployed.loc[:, False] = (1 - bfs_without_unemployed.loc[:, True]).values

Fixing Methodology¶

Expected Counts¶

In [34]:
%%time 
marginals = marginal_fitting.compute_ist_vs_scenario_marginal_summary_table(persons, persons_ist,
                                                                            YEAR, YEAR_IST, 
                                                                            'current_job_rank', 
                                                                            ['age']
                                                                           )

2020-04-22 10:12:20,387 - INFO - synpop.marginal_fitting: The following segments have no data in ist and will stay untouched: Int64Index([110, 111, 112, 113, 114, 116, 117, 118, 119, 120, 121, 122, 123,
            124, 125, 126, 127, 128, 129, 131, 132],
           dtype='int64', name='age')

Wall time: 22.2 s

Adapting the marginals to match BFS predictions¶

Note: Scaling up the 2017 proportions on the 2040 age pyramid is not enough:

  • BFS predicts that more people will be working in 2040 (e.g. more women should be employed)
  • The control totals for non-null job ranks have to be scaled up to match the BFS prediction for the number of active people
  • Since we do not know much about how apprentice will evove, it is kept stable in this post fixing step.
  • Control totals for management and employee are modulated for the number of null predicted by the BFS to be mached.
In [35]:
original_nulls = marginals.reset_index().query('current_job_rank == "null"').set_index('age')[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].rename('original_nulls') 
original_counts = marginals.reset_index().groupby('age')[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].sum().rename('original_counts') 

original_null_ratio = (original_nulls / original_counts).rename('original_null_ratio')
bfs_pred_ratio = bfs_without_unemployed.reset_index().query('~is_employed').set_index('age')[f'expected_ratios_{YEAR}'].rename('predicted_null_ratio')
_ = pd.concat([original_null_ratio, bfs_pred_ratio],axis=1).plot()
In [36]:
marginals_adapted = marginals.drop([f'deltas_{YEAR}'], axis=1).copy(deep=True)

Setting the nulls to what would fit the BFS prediction

In [37]:
expected_nulls = (marginals.reset_index().groupby('age')[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].sum().rename('original_counts') * bfs_pred_ratio).astype(int)
delta_nulls = (original_nulls - expected_nulls).rename('delta_nulls')
original_employees = marginals_adapted.reset_index().query('current_job_rank == "employee"').set_index('age')[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].rename('original_employees') 
original_management = marginals_adapted.reset_index().query('current_job_rank == "management"').set_index('age')[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].rename('original_management')
In [38]:
# Because we componsate "nulls" only by "employee" or "management" (not "apprentice"), some age groups cannot be fixed perfectly.
# This is not really a problem, the quality is already much better with this simple-ish fix.
delta_nulls_bounded = -pd.concat([(original_management + original_employees), -delta_nulls], axis=1).min(axis=1)

# Adtitionally if there are 0 "employee" and 0 "management", none can be created! 
# i.e. age 16, 17 which have only apprentice!
delta_nulls_bounded = delta_nulls_bounded.mask((original_management + original_employees) == 0, 0)
delta_nulls_bounded = delta_nulls_bounded.rename('delta_nulls_bounded')

_ = pd.concat([delta_nulls, delta_nulls_bounded], axis=1).plot()
In [39]:
adapted_nulls = (original_nulls - delta_nulls_bounded).rename('adapted_nulls')

mask_nulls = marginals_adapted.index.get_level_values(1) == 'null'
marginals_adapted[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'] = marginals_adapted[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].mask(mask_nulls, adapted_nulls)

Compensating the difference in "null" with "employee" and "management"

In [40]:
# The ratio of employee over all employee & management defines how much employees marginals will be adapted.
employee_management_ratio = (original_employees / (original_employees + original_management)).fillna(0)
delta_employee = (delta_nulls_bounded * employee_management_ratio).round(0).astype(int)
delta_management = delta_nulls_bounded - delta_employee
In [41]:
# At this point we have already made sure there are enough "employee" and "management" 
# This is why delta_nulls is bounded.
adapted_employees = (original_employees + delta_employee).rename('adapted_employees')

mask_employee = marginals_adapted.index.get_level_values(1) == 'employee'
marginals_adapted[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'] = marginals_adapted[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].mask(mask_employee, adapted_employees)
In [42]:
adapted_management = (original_management + delta_management).rename('adapted_management')

mask_management = marginals_adapted.index.get_level_values(1) == 'management'
marginals_adapted[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'] = marginals_adapted[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].mask(mask_management, adapted_management)
In [44]:
# Sanity check: 
bfs_employed_prediction = (marginals.reset_index().groupby('age')[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].sum().rename('original_counts') * (1-bfs_pred_ratio)).astype(int).rename('marginals')
_ = pd.concat([adapted_employees, 
               (adapted_employees + adapted_management).rename('adapted employees & management'), 
               bfs_employed_prediction
              ], 
              axis=1).plot()
In [45]:
# Sanity check:
adapted_nulls = marginals_adapted.reset_index().query('current_job_rank == "null"').set_index('age')[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].rename('adapted_nulls') 
adapted_counts = marginals_adapted.reset_index().groupby('age')[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].sum().rename('adapted_counts') 

original_null_ratio = (adapted_nulls / adapted_counts).rename('adapted_null_ratio')
bfs_pred_ratio = bfs_without_unemployed.reset_index().query('~is_employed').set_index('age')[f'expected_ratios_{YEAR}'].rename('predicted_null_ratio')
_ = pd.concat([original_null_ratio, bfs_pred_ratio],axis=1).plot()
In [46]:
# Sanity check:
pop_per_age_before = marginals.groupby(level='age').sum()[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}']
pop_per_age_after = marginals_adapted.groupby(level='age').sum()[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}']

assert ((pop_per_age_after - pop_per_age_before) == 0).all(), 'The pop per age has changes after applying the new control totals!'
In [47]:
expected_pop_counts = (marginals_adapted[f'expected_counts_{YEAR}'].rename('expected_counts')
                       .reset_index()
                       .pivot(index='age', columns='current_job_rank', values='expected_counts')
                       )[ORDERED_CATEGORIES]
In [48]:
expected_pop_counts.head()
Out[48]:
current_job_rank apprentice employee management null
age
0 0 0 0 89500
1 0 0 0 90094
2 0 0 0 91606
3 0 0 0 92288
4 0 0 0 92272

Probabiltiy Model¶

In [49]:
ct_job_ist = marginal_fitting.compute_cross_table(persons_ist,
                                                  main_feature='current_job_rank',
                                                  secondary_features=['current_edu', 'is_employed']
                                                  )
In [50]:
ct_job_ist

person_proba = marginal_fitting.build_person_proba_with_from_cross_table(
    persons, ct_job_ist, 
    merge_on=['current_edu', 'is_employed']
)

Wall time: 12.2 s

Fixing...¶

The logging can be set to INFO or DEBUG (at the top of the notebook logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)) based on how much detail is needed!

In [53]:
fixed_job = marginal_fitting.fix_categorical_feature(persons=persons,
                                                     feature='current_job_rank',
                                                     pop_segment_variables=['age'],
                                                     control_totals=expected_pop_counts,
                                                     person_proba=person_proba
                                                     )

In [54]:
persons_fixed = persons.copy(deep=True)
persons_fixed = persons_fixed.drop('current_job_rank', axis=1)
persons_fixed = pd.merge(persons_fixed, fixed_job, left_on='person_id', right_index=True)

persons_fixed.shape
Out[54]:
(10037464, 16)

Examining SynPop+¶

Global Counts¶

In [55]:
%%time
summary_table_fixed = marginal_fitting.compute_comparison_summary(persons_fixed, persons_ist, 
                                                                  YEAR, YEAR_IST, 
                                                                  groupby='current_job_rank')

Wall time: 17.1 s
In [56]:
print('As reference, the total population growth is: {:.2f}'.format(persons_fixed.shape[0] / persons_ist.shape[0]))
summary_table_fixed

As reference, the total population growth is: 1.17
Out[56]:
counts_2017 counts_2040 growth prop_2017 prop_2040
current_job_rank
apprentice 184450 204099 1.11 0.02 0.02
employee 2962016 3146851 1.06 0.35 0.31
management 1680773 1835556 1.09 0.20 0.18
null 3732912 4850958 1.30 0.44 0.48

Marginals Per Age¶

In [57]:
%%time 
marginals_fixed = marginal_fitting.compute_ist_vs_scenario_marginal_counts(persons_fixed, persons_ist,
                                                                           YEAR, YEAR_IST, 
                                                                           feature='current_job_rank', 
                                                                           control_level_list=['age'])

Wall time: 18.8 s

Fixed SynPop

In [58]:
counts_per_cat_fixed = (marginals_fixed
                        .reset_index()
                        .pivot(index='age', columns='current_job_rank', values=f'counts_{YEAR}')
                        )
counts_per_cat_fixed = counts_per_cat_fixed[ORDERED_CATEGORIES]
In [59]:
title = f'Current Job Rank - SynPop{YEAR}+ (SMART)'

ax = plot_multi_class_feature_per_age(counts_per_cat_fixed, colour_dict=COLOUR_DICT, ymax=150_000, title=title)

save_figure(SAVE_OUTPUTS, f'04_SynPop{YEAR}Plus_current_job_rank_by_age_SMART.{FIG_FORMAT}',
            OUTPUT_DIR, FIG_DPI, FIG_FORMAT, FIG_BBOX_INCHES, FIG_PAD_INCHES)

2020-04-22 10:14:30,633 - INFO - root: Figure saved to file : ./outputs\04_SynPop2040Plus_current_job_rank_by_age_SMART.png

Comment on the Results:

  • We achieved what we need for the control total to match later on
  • The BFS predictions cannot be matched exaclty because we are not scaling apprentice
  • Keeping apprentice stable is done to stay close to the 2017 sctucture.

Cross-Relationships¶

Fixed SynPop

CT1

In [60]:
ax, _ = plot_ct1(persons_fixed, title=f'(04) CT1: SynPop{YEAR}+ (SMART)')

save_figure(SAVE_OUTPUTS, f'04_CT1_SynPop{YEAR}Plus_SMART.{FIG_FORMAT}',
            OUTPUT_DIR, FIG_DPI, FIG_FORMAT, FIG_BBOX_INCHES, FIG_PAD_INCHES)

Saving Results¶

In [63]:
OUTPUT_DIR
Out[63]:
'./outputs'
In [ ]:
%%time
if SAVE_OUTPUTS:
    results_file = os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, 'fixed_current_job_rank_SMART.pickle.gzip')
    fixed_job.to_pickle(results_file, compression='gzip')
